# Transcrição: 01-Why Customer Avatars Are Key for Social

- **Fonte:** 01-Why Customer Avatars Are Key for Social
- **Duração:** 30:27

## [00:00 - 03:04]

What's going on everybody? Laura here with you for module three of Train My Social Media Manager. So today we are going to talk about avatars and not these ones, although I do love this ride at Disney and this is a really great movie and I would like to know if anyone else likes it so we can chat about it a little bit. But we're not going to be talking about these avatars, we're going to be talking about the avatar for your brand. So who is the person you are speaking to when you create content, when you create ads, when you create emails, when you create anything that you're putting out into the world from your brand, who is it that you are speaking to? And the reason we want to really figure this out is because using an avatar makes creating your content and as I said every other piece of your marketing a whole lot easier. If you know who you're speaking to, if you really have a clear view of the person, some people even give them specific names. I know companies that will name their avatar, they'll figure out where they live, what they do all day, what books they read. You can go really, really deep into creating your avatar. But we're going to show you today, what I want to show you right now is kind of a real life example of how some companies use their avatar to create their content. So first let's talk about Boom by Cindy Joseph, which is the cosmetic and skincare line in which I run the social media for. And our avatar here is women over 50 who are experiencing aging in this society. We're often told that aging is bad, wrinkles are bad, gray hair is bad, we want to cover those up. So Boom as a company, we want to speak to women who are experiencing this, who are experiencing those things being thrown at them in society. They're talking about anti-age, they're being told that they're not good as they are, and that is the number one kind of definition of our avatar. And then we also are talking to women who care about sustainability. We try really hard in our company to focus on sustainability whenever we can in our packaging and our ingredients and things like that. We want someone who, or our avatar is someone who wants quality ingredients in their skincare. They also want to celebrate and embrace aging, whether that is someone who is already doing this or someone that wants to embody that mindset and start to celebrate and embrace aging. And also someone who wants their makeup to enhance rather than cover up. So this is obviously a very short list of what our avatar is. We could go, like I said, really, really deep. We could go into what books they read, really create a whole personality around the person that we want to speak with. But I'm going to keep this one short and sweet just to kind of give you that overview of how this helps us create our content.

## [03:04 - 06:08]

So once we have our avatar, we're then able to come up with those, our goals for our content, right? Once we know who we're speaking to, we can say, what do we want to say to them in our content? And a couple of those things are we want to offer a pro-age viewpoint instead of anti-age, which is actually a lot more difficult than it sounds. People are kind of inundated with this anti-age viewpoint. So bringing in a pro-age stance is a lot harder to get through to people than you would imagine. We also want to share the idea that aging doesn't have to be a bad thing. It's happening to everybody, so why would we kind of demonize it and say it's a bad thing and try to cover it up and try to stop it? It's going to happen to everyone. We want to have a discussion about beauty and what it means. What it means now versus what it meant maybe when they were 20, 30 years old versus what it means at 60, 70, 80, 90. We want to share tips on self-care and skin care. We want to inspire confidence in aging with our content. We want to be a platform for women to share their own stories about wrinkles, about silver hair, about their life experiences and so things like that. And then we also want to talk about the value of our products and our ingredients, obviously, because in the end we are trying to sell a product. So now that we know who we're talking to and what we want to say to them, we are able to create this bank of content surrounding all of those things. Four all-natural ingredients for your skin will love. Five makeup tips for older women, how to shift your perspective on aging, how to make the switch to all-natural skin care if you don't already have all-natural skin care. Six tips for taking amazing photos and videos. We actually did that one right when the COVID pandemic hit, so it was very relevant at the time for video calls and things like that that were happening a lot more often. Creating your own spot at home, three things. Every woman needs to hear about aging and four tips for overcoming perfectionism. So we're really speaking to this woman who is over 50 who cares about sustainability and the ingredients in her products. She wants to overcome perfectionism and talk about pro-age. So we know that we are speaking to her and we put that through in our content. And this also impacts what we put out on our social media. So we have Boom by Cindy Joseph's Instagram right here. You can see we have quotes. We created that platform for women to tell their stories. So most of the women on here are actually not models. They are our ambassadors in creating user-generated content for us. We do talk a little bit about our products. We have reviews for our products. And this goes for all of our social media platforms. Instagram is just the easiest one for me to show in kind of this type of setting. So that's why I'm showing you this one. But you see that all of those pieces of content inform what we do on social media as well.

## [06:08 - 09:09]

So let's talk about another company. Let's look at REI. We can assume REI's customer avatar based on the content they put out. And I did some research into what they're doing in the world. So we know that REI, their avatar is most likely outdoor enthusiasts. They care about the environment. They care about the quality of gear they're using. They like to share their adventures. So they're very social people in general. And they generally live a healthy lifestyle. And this one I thought was really interesting that I found is they like IPAs. So even though they generally live a healthy lifestyle, these are the kind of people that you will see at the bottom of a mountain at a brewery or a winery. So I thought it was pretty interesting and they do create content around that, which is pretty neat. So what that does is it informs, once they have this customer avatar, that informs the content that they want to put out. So they want to educate on the environment because they know their avatar cares about the environment. They want to educate on environmental policies for that same reason. They want to provide inspiration. So maybe a new thing to do outdoors or create inspiration around getting better at whatever activity that you do normally. They want to encourage responsibility in nature. So they put out a lot of content around how to be a better either conservationist or environmentalist or just, you know, person outside on how to be responsible. They entertain a lot with cooking. They do a lot. They know that their avatar typically eats local. They eat healthy. They shop at farmers markets and things like that. So they do a lot of entertaining with cooking type content. And they also showcase thought leaders in their space. So they'll do a lot of interviews with environmentalists or people who, you know, have a lot to do with the outdoor space. Then they talk about their gear, the perfect kit for camping, for camp cooking, the best hiking gear for dogs. So they're really putting out a lot of different types of content. They even do more than this. REI does. They do a lot of interviews with thought leaders in the space. A lot of gear type things. Some how-tos. They do a lot of content around that as well. And that informs their social media. So they focus a lot on user-generated content, REI does, for their social media.

## [15:15 - 18:16]

And yes, using avatars makes creating content easier. Absolutely, positively does. And from the perspective of the social media manager, it does it makes things so much easier when you understand the avatar, who you are talking to. But on the client side, and on the business owner side, it connects content to business objectives. And this is how you end up making your clients very happy, making the person you're working for very happy or affecting your own business. If you are doing the social media for your own business, connecting social media marketing and the content that you're producing to business objectives is absolutely critical. What we want to be doing when we're producing social media content is we want to be creating assets for the business. So what do I mean by that? Well, we want to be creating content. We're going to spend time on that. We're going to spend energy on that. We might spend money on that. We want to be producing something that is an asset for the business. So we want things that will meet business objectives like increasing awareness and engagement for the brands, the people, you know, the things that we sell. We want more people to know about it. We want to establish trust and authority in the market. This is really critical for business to business, but also B2C as well. I mean, business to consumer, we want to establish authority and trust that we know what we're talking about. And this will be nowhere is this more important than, for example, in service-based businesses that are B2B. They need to be trusted. For example, let's take an attorney or an accountant or real estate professional. They are looking to become, you know, they want the market to know that they are trustworthy and in a high authority in the marketplace. They want to generate leads or grow their email list. This is what your clients want. This is what your business wants. This might be what your employer wants. And we want to make sure that our social media marketing is connecting back to these kinds of objectives like converting leads into customers. Can we create content that does that? Absolutely. We can sell more to the customers we already have. So we've got customers. How do we cross sell them or upsell them into other solutions that we have and then retaining customers that we have? So if we're, you know, this is nowhere is this more critical, for example, than in the software space where everything has moved pretty much to software as a service. And so we pay month over month. We have to be able to retain those customers. Same with CPG brands. You know, they want you to buy again and again and again. So when we can connect our social media marketing to marketing objectives, now we're really on to something.
